title: Building a Project Management / Kanban App in Laravel
subtitle: Boards, cards, drag-and-drop ordering, real-time collaboration, and an activity feed — the Laravel architecture behind a Jira/Trello-style tool.

sections.h2: How a Kanban app fits together

blocks.p: A project-management app is a hierarchy of orderable things that many people edit at once. The entire design pressure comes from two facts: cards must keep a stable, user-defined order, and several users drag them around simultaneously. Get ordering and concurrency right and the rest is CRUD.
The canonical hierarchy:

blocks.diagram:
Workspace
 └─ Project (members, roles)
     └─ Board (a Kanban view)
         └─ Column / List  ("To Do", "In Progress", "Done")
             └─ Card / Issue  (title, assignees, labels, position)
                 ├─ Comments
                 ├─ Checklist items
                 ├─ Attachments
                 └─ Activity log entries

blocks.p: The request lifecycle for the one action that matters most — moving a card:

blocks.diagram:
User drags card  ──►  PATCH /cards/{id}/move {column_id, after_id}
                         │
                         ▼
          Policy: can user edit this board?
                         │
                         ▼
          Compute new position (fractional / lexorank)
                         │
                         ▼
          Persist column_id + position  (single UPDATE)
                         │
             ┌───────────┴───────────┐
             ▼                       ▼
     Broadcast CardMoved        Write ActivityLog
     to board channel           (queued)
             ▼
     Other users' boards update live

blocks.p: The four subsystems you keep returning to:

ul: Domain model — workspace → project → board → column → card, plus labels, comments, attachments.
Ordering — how a card knows it sits between two others, cheaply, under concurrent moves.
Authorization — project membership and roles decide who can see/edit what.
Real-time + activity — broadcasting changes to other viewers and recording an audit trail.

blocks.callout: A Kanban app is an ordered tree edited concurrently. The defining engineering problem is card ordering under simultaneous drag-and-drop — solve that with fractional/lexorank positions and you avoid full-column renumbering and most race conditions.
blocks.title: The mental model in one sentence

blocks.p: A board belongs to a project and hasMany columns; a column hasMany cards. A card belongs to a column and carries a position. Cards relate to users through an assignees pivot and to labels through a pivot. This is a straightforward relational tree — the subtlety is entirely in position.

The naive approach gives each card an integer 1,2,3… Dropping a card at the top means renumbering every card below it — many writes, and two concurrent moves easily produce duplicate or conflicting numbers. It does not scale and races badly.

Instead, store a position that lets you insert between two neighbours without touching anyone else. Two common schemes: (1) fractional float — new position = midpoint of the neighbours' positions (e.g. between 1.0 and 2.0 → 1.5); (2) lexorank — a base-N string (e.g. "aaa", "aam", "aaz") where you generate a string lexicographically between two others. A move becomes a single UPDATE of one row. Periodic rebalancing handles precision exhaustion.

Access is driven by project membership with a role (owner/admin/member/viewer). A Laravel Policy on the Board/Card models checks the acting user's role on the parent project. Centralize it: every board/card query is scoped to projects the user belongs to, and every mutating route runs through the policy.

When one user moves a card, others watching the board should see it move. Laravel broadcasting (Reverb/Pusher/Ably) pushes a CardMoved event to a private channel named per board (e.g. board.42); the channel authorization callback re-uses the same membership check. The frontend listens and reorders locally.

The drag feels instant because the frontend moves the card immediately (optimistic update) then sends the request. If the server rejects it (lost permission, stale board) the UI rolls back. The server is the source of truth for final position; the broadcast reconciles everyone.

Every meaningful change (card created, moved, assigned, commented) appends an immutable activity row: actor, verb, subject, and a small JSON payload. Write it from model events or explicitly, and queue it so it never slows the user's action. The feed is just a paginated query of this table.
Comments belongTo a card and a user. An @mention parses usernames, links them, and fires a Laravel Notification (database + mail/broadcast) to the mentioned user. Notifications are queued.

blocks.callout: The number-one mistake is integer positions with renumber-on-insert: it generates O(n) writes per move and races under concurrency. Use fractional or lexorank positions so a move is a single-row update.
blocks.title: Most common misconception

blocks.p: A Kanban app is an ordered tree of cards in columns, edited by many users at once; fractional/lexorank positions make moves cheap, policies guard access, and broadcasting keeps everyone's board in sync.

Picture a physical whiteboard with sticky notes in columns. Many people stand at it moving notes. Integer positions are like numbering every note and rewriting all the numbers whenever one moves — chaos when two hands move at once. Fractional positions are like saying "put it halfway between these two" — you only touch the one note. The policy is the room's lock: only team members get in. Broadcasting is everyone seeing the same board update the instant a note moves.

blocks.diagram:
Column "To Do"      position
 ┌──────────┐
 │ Card A    │  1.0
 │ Card B    │  2.0   ◄── drop new card here, between A and B
 │ Card C    │  3.0
 └──────────┘
 new position = (1.0 + 2.0) / 2 = 1.5    → one UPDATE, nobody else touched

blocks.p: Each lesson is one idea. Code is illustrative, not copy-paste production.

blocks.h3: Lesson 1 — Model the tree with clear relationships

blocks.p: Compute the midpoint of the neighbours at the drop target. New top = first/2; new bottom = last+1; between = (prev+next)/2.

blocks.h3: Lesson 4 — Guard with a policy + scoped queries
Lesson 5 — Broadcast on a private board channel
Lesson 6 — Queue the activity log so it never blocks

blocks.code:
// fire-and-forget audit trail
Activity::queue(
    actor: $r->user(),
    verb: 'moved_card',
    subject: $card,
    payload: ['to_column' => $card->column_id],
);  // dispatched to a queue; the user's move already returned

blocks.h3: Lesson 7 — Rebalance when precision runs out

blocks.p: After many midpoint inserts, floats converge (1.5, 1.25, 1.125…). When the gap between neighbours falls below a threshold, run a queued job that rewrites the whole column's positions to clean integers (1,2,3…). Rare, cheap, invisible to users.
Answer before revealing — recall effort builds memory.

items.q: Q1. Why are integer positions with renumber-on-insert a poor ordering strategy?
items.a: Inserting at the top renumbers every card below (O(n) writes), and two concurrent moves can produce duplicate or conflicting numbers. It neither scales nor handles concurrency.

items.q: Q2. How does a fractional position make a move cheap?
items.a: You set the moved card's position to the midpoint of its two new neighbours — a single-row UPDATE that touches no other card. Order is preserved by the numeric value.

items.q: Q3. What is the failure mode of fractional positions, and the fix?
items.a: Repeated midpoint inserts exhaust float precision (1.5 → 1.25 → 1.125 …). Fix: when the neighbour gap is below a threshold, run a queued rebalance job that rewrites the column's positions to clean integers.

items.q: Q4. Where does authorization live for moving a card, and what does it check?
items.a: In a Policy (e.g. CardPolicy@update) invoked by the controller. It checks that the user is a member of the card's board's project with a role that permits editing (owner/admin/member, not viewer).

items.q: Q5. How do other users see a card move in real time, and how is the channel secured?
items.a: The server broadcasts a CardMoved event to a private channel named per board (board.{id}). The channel authorization callback re-uses the project-membership check, so only members receive it. Use ->toOthers() to skip the actor who already updated optimistically.

items.q: Q6. Why queue the activity-log write instead of doing it inline?
items.a: The audit entry isn't needed for the user's action to succeed; queuing it keeps the move response fast and decouples the feed from the hot path. The feed is then a paginated read of the activity table.

items.q: Q7. What is optimistic UI here, and what must the client handle?
items.a: The frontend moves the card immediately, then sends the request. It must roll back if the server rejects (lost permission, stale state) and reconcile with the authoritative broadcast/response.

blocks.p: Re-do the Principle 5 quiz from memory and re-derive the move diagram at each session.

head: When
Session (~10–15 min)
Goal
rows: Read packet; quiz with answers open.
Quiz closed-book; redraw the card-move request flow.
Flashcards; explain fractional vs lexorank aloud with trade-offs.
Interleaved quiz; write the move() controller from blank.
Re-derive the policy + broadcast channel from memory.
Teach the ordering problem to someone in 5 minutes.
Closed-book interleaved pass; missed items restart at Day 1.

blocks.callout: Retrieving just before you'd forget produces the strongest gain. Easy, too-frequent review feels productive but builds little.
blocks.title: Why the gaps grow

blocks.p: Topics shuffled so you practise choosing the right idea, not pattern-matching a section.

items.q: M1. A user reports cards occasionally jump to the wrong order after a teammate drags at the same moment. Likely cause?
items.a: Positions computed from stale client-sent neighbour values, or integer renumbering racing. Recompute the midpoint from current DB neighbours inside the move handler (ideally in a transaction), and prefer fractional/lexorank so concurrent inserts don't collide.

items.q: M2. Board updates stop appearing live for one user but moves still save. Where do you look first?
items.a: Broadcasting/channel auth, not the database. Check the private channel authorization for board.{id} (membership callback), the websocket connection (Reverb/Pusher), and that CardMoved implements ShouldBroadcast and is dispatched.

items.q: M3. The activity feed is slow and the board feels laggy on busy projects. Two fixes?
items.a: (1) Queue activity writes so they're off the move hot path. (2) Paginate/limit and index the activity table (board_id, created_at); consider a separate read model. Don't eager-load the whole history.

items.q: M4. After months, one column's cards refuse to reorder cleanly. Diagnosis?
items.a: Float precision exhausted from repeated midpoint inserts — neighbour gap underflowed. Run the queued rebalance job to rewrite that column's positions to clean integers; lower the rebalance threshold.

items.q: M5. A viewer-role user can move cards via the API though the UI hides the control. Flaw?
items.a: Authorization enforced only in the UI. The move route must call the Policy server-side; never rely on hidden frontend controls. Add a policy test for the viewer role.

items.q: M6. When would lexorank be worth its extra complexity over fractional floats?
items.a: Very long-lived columns with extremely frequent reordering where float precision would exhaust quickly, or when you want human-inspectable, collision-resistant string ranks and controlled rebalancing. For most apps, fractional floats + occasional rebalance are simpler and sufficient.

ul: Build the move endpoint twice — once with fractional floats, once with a lexorank library. Feeling both teaches the trade-off better than the table.
Break it on purpose — switch to integer renumbering and reproduce the concurrency bug; then watch fractional positions fix it.
Write the authorization tests first on your next feature: viewer cannot move, non-member cannot subscribe to the channel.
Add real-time presence — show who's viewing the board (presence channels) and live cursors.
Stretch goals: swimlanes, WIP limits per column, card dependencies, full-text search (Scout), saved filters, and an undo built on the activity log.

flashcards: The defining engineering problem of a Kanban app
Card ordering under concurrent drag-and-drop. Solve with fractional/lexorank positions so a move is one UPDATE.
Why integer positions fail
Insert-at-top renumbers everything below (O(n) writes) and races to duplicate numbers under concurrency.
Fractional position for a new top card
first_position / 2
Fractional position between two cards
(prev_position + next_position) / 2 — single-row update, nobody else touched.
Failure mode of fractional floats + fix
Precision exhausts after repeated midpoints; run a queued rebalance to rewrite the column to clean integers.
Lexorank, in one line
Base-N string ranks ("aaa","aam","aaz") generated lexicographically between neighbours; rebalance when strings grow.
Where card authorization lives
A Policy checking project membership + role on the card's board's project — enforced server-side, not in the UI.
How others see a card move live
Broadcast CardMoved to a private per-board channel (board.{id}); channel auth re-uses the membership check.
Purpose of ->toOthers()
Skip broadcasting back to the actor, who already updated optimistically.
Optimistic UI + its obligation
Move the card immediately client-side; roll back if the server rejects and reconcile with the authoritative result.
Why queue the activity log
Audit entry isn't needed for the action to succeed; queuing keeps the move fast and decouples the feed.
@mention → notification path
Parse mention, link the user, fire a queued Laravel Notification (database + mail/broadcast).
When to choose lexorank over floats
Very long-lived, heavily reordered columns or when you want collision-resistant, inspectable string ranks.
glossary: Board
A Kanban view belonging to a project; has many columns.
Column / List
An ordered bucket of cards (e.g. To Do, Done).
Card / Issue
A unit of work with title, assignees, labels, and a position.
Position
The orderable value (float or lexorank string) that places a card among its siblings.
Fractional indexing
Ordering by inserting at the numeric midpoint between neighbours.
Lexorank
String-based rank scheme allowing insertion between two ranks lexicographically.
Rebalance
Periodic rewrite of a column's positions to restore spacing/precision.
Policy
Laravel authorization class deciding if a user may act on a model.
Private channel
A broadcast channel requiring server-side authorization to subscribe.
Optimistic UI
Applying a change on the client before the server confirms, rolling back on failure.
Activity log
Immutable audit trail of actor/verb/subject/payload rows powering the feed.
Presence channel
A broadcast channel that also tracks who is currently subscribed.
